original_abstract: This paper explores the alignment between higher education curricula and the demands of the modern job market, focusing specifically on a Professional Bachelor program in Applied Foreign Language Studies at a Moroccan university. Utilizing a mixed-method research design, the study evaluates the integration of essential 21st-century skills—such as digital literacy, critical thinking, and problem-solving—through curriculum analysis, student surveys, and faculty interviews. The findings reveal a significant discrepancy between academic preparation and professional requirements. Curriculum evaluation and student feedback indicate that many courses remain theoretical, redundant, and poorly suited to fostering employability. In contrast, faculty members generally perceived the curriculum as effective, often attributing students' lack of readiness to external factors like absenteeism. Systematic triangulation of the data highlights an urgent need for a holistic curriculum revamp. The study recommends transitioning toward student-centered, project-based learning and strengthening industry partnerships to ensure graduates possess the resilience and technical proficiency required for the evolving global economy. The paper concludes with a proposed framework for continuous curriculum review to bridge the persistent gap between academic output and workforce expectations.
humanized_abstract: This paper investigates the relationship between the curriculum of higher education institutions and the labour market, using the example of a Professional Bachelor’s degree in Applied Foreign Language Studies in one of the Moroccan universities. We used a mixed-methods approach in order to assess the presence of some of the essential skills for the 21st century (such as digital literacy, critical and analytical skills, and creative problem-solving) in the curriculum of the programme through the analysis of the structure and content of the subjects offered, the opinions of the students and lecturers. The results of the study are revealing a discrepancy between the competences acquired by students and the requirements of the labour market. Thus, the curriculum analysis and the students’ opinions have shown that a large number of disciplines have a strongly theoretical and repetitive character and do not contribute to preparing students for employment. The opinions of lecturers were however more positive regarding the curriculum, attributing the insufficient preparation of graduates to factors other than the curriculum such as absenteeism from classes, etc. A thorough triangulation of the results obtained shows the need for a comprehensive reform of the curriculum of the Programme. In this respect, we recommend a studentcentred approach based on project learning, as well as the strengthening of relations with the employers’ sector. Also, graduates need to acquire more flexibility and a higher level of technical competence, in line with the characteristics of the contemporary global economy, for which we propose a model for the continuous evaluation of the curriculum and its adjustment to the needs of the labour market.
